title: Multiple Superconducting Phases in Palladium Deuteride Induced by Nuclear-Spin Isotope Effect
authors: Ryoma Kato, Ten-ichiro Yoshida, Riku Iimori, Masanobu Shiga, Yuji Inagaki, Takashi Kimura, Koichiro Ienaga, Tatsuya Kawae
abstract: We study the superconducting properties of high-quality PdD$_{x}$ films. The resistivity shows a sharp drop at $T$ $\sim$1.7 K, marking the superconducting transition. However, a finite resistivity persists and vanishes at $\sim$0.6 K. The temperature and magnetic-field dependences of the resistivity exhibit multiple anomalies within the superconducting state, revealing distinct superconducting phases. Such anomalies are absent in PdH$_{x}$ films. These results demonstrate a clear qualitative difference between the superconducting phase diagrams of PdD$_{x}$ and PdH$_{x}$, highlighting the role of nuclear-spin isotope effects.
summary: This paper demonstrates that palladium deuteride exhibits multiple superconducting phases characterized by a unique resistivity anomaly, which is absent in palladium hydride. The researchers attribute this difference to the nuclear-spin isotope effect, suggesting that the superconducting order parameter in PdDx may have non-s-wave symmetry.
main_problem: Investigating the origin of multiple superconducting phases in palladium deuteride (PdDx) and determining whether the difference from palladium hydride (PdHx) arises from mass-dependent tunneling or nuclear-spin isotope effects.
main_result: The study identifies a unique rectangular-shaped resistivity anomaly in PdDx that is absent in PdHx, concluding that the effect is driven by the nuclear-spin isotope effect (I=1 for D vs. I=1/2 for H) and suggesting non-s-wave pairing.
method: The researchers used low-temperature absorption to prepare high-quality PdDx and PdHx thin films and performed temperature- and magnetic-field-dependent electrical resistivity measurements.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of high-quality palladium deuteride (PdDx) and palladium hydride (PdHx) thin films where hydrogen/deuterium atoms occupy interstitial sites in the palladium lattice.
key_observables: Electrical resistivity (rho-T and rho-H), superconducting transition temperatures (Tc1, Tc2), and characteristic magnetic field values (HLC, HAL, HAH).
important_parameters: Nuclear spin (I=1 for deuterium, I=1/2 for hydrogen), deuterium concentration (x), and temperature/magnetic field ranges.
main_assumptions: The authors assume the observed anomalies are intrinsic to the PdDx system rather than due to sample inhomogeneity or impurities.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows resistivity-temperature curves highlighting the rectangular anomaly; Fig 3 shows resistivity-magnetic field dependence at various temperatures; Fig 4 presents the T-H phase diagrams comparing PdDx and PdHx.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of multiple phases in PdDx, describes the experimental preparation of films, presents resistivity measurements showing the anomaly, compares PdDx to PdHx to isolate the nuclear-spin effect, and concludes with a theoretical hypothesis regarding non-s-wave pairing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Temperature dependence of the resistivity in PdDx film for x = 0.792, 0.795, and 0.804. The inset shows the low-temperature behavior. A rectangular-shaped anomaly appears in the residual-resistivity region. For instance, TRL = 1.14 K and TRH = 1.36 K, as indicated by arrows, in PdD0.804.

FIG. 2: (a) The overall features for magnetic field dependence of the resistivity in the PdD0.804 film. The transition curve around Tc1 is broadened with increasing the magnetic field. (b) Magnetic field dependence of the resistivity between 2000 Oe and 3000 Oe, where magnetic fields are shown in increments of 200 Oe. A clear bend is visible at Tb in the temperature dependence of the resistivity, as indicated by arrows, between H = 2200 Oe and 2800 Oe. (c) Magnetic field dependence of the rectangular-shaped anomaly. (d) Magnetic field dependence of the resistivity around Tc2. A sharp drop in resistivity can be observed up to H = 400 Oe, whereas zero resistivity is no longer observed at 500...

Fig. 3(b) highlights the low-field region. Several anoma- lies are clearly observed in the ρ – H curves below T ∼1.0 K. At T ∼0.6 K, as the magnetic field increases, the resistivity rises steeply at H ∼500 Oe and reaches a nearly constant value of ∼1.76 µΩcm at HLC ∼610 Oe, which is consistent with the residual resistivity below the rectangular-shaped anomaly. Subsequently, the resistiv- ity remains nearly constant and then exhibits another sudden increase at HAL ∼1800 Oe. Notably, the magni- tude of this jump is ∼0.17 µΩcm, which is in good agree- ment with the resistivity drop at TRL associated with the rectangular-shaped anomaly. These observations suggest that the anomaly can be induced...

FIG. 3: (a) An overview on the magnetic-field dependence of the resistivity at T ∼0.6 K, 0.8 K, 1.0 K, 1.2 K, and 1.4 K in the PdD0.804 film. A clear bend appears at HAH in the magnetic field dependence of the resistivity below the upper critical field, HUC, as indicated by arrows. (b) The low-field region is extended. The resistivity at T ∼0.6 K, 0.8 K, and 1.0 K increases sharply at HAL, as indicated by arrows, as the magnetic field increases.
FIG. 4: (a) The T - H phase diagram of the PdD0.804 film. and PdH0.870 film (b) [20]. The blue and red plots are ob- tained based on the ρ – T and ρ – H measurements.
